macro_segments.title: Bird Chatas vs. Bird Olah: Differences in Melikah and Blood Application

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the daf and the core distinction between bird chatas and bird olah regarding simanim and blood handling
Rabbi Eliezer bar Rabbi Shimon's position that severing both simanim in a chatas does not invalidate it
Analysis of whether treating a chatas like an olah (by cutting both simanim) makes it invalid because it becomes like an olah service

macro_segments.title: The Requirement of Rov Basar (Majority of Flesh) During Melikah of a Chatas Bird

micro_segments.title: Inference from the debate that you must cut the majority of the flesh during the melikah of a chatas
Baraita explaining that melikah requires cutting through the spinal cord without cutting away the majority of flesh until reaching one of the simanim
Application of rov basar requirement: for chatas you cut one siman and complete the flesh-cutting in that act; for olah you must cut both simanim before rov basar
Discussion of shehiya (pause) between cutting the simanim and whether pausing after the first siman before cutting the second would invalidate an olah

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yirmiyah's Alternative Explanation of Rabbi Eliezer bar Rabbi Shimon's Position on Lo Yavdil

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yirmiyah presents a tradition from Rabbi Shimon ben Lakish regarding Rabbi Eliezer bar Rabbi Shimon's actual reasoning
Rabbi Eliezer bar Rabbi Shimon's explanation: lo yavdil means 'not required' rather than 'forbidden,' making severance optional
Parallel to the well-covering case (bor): context determines whether lo means prohibition or lack of requirement
How the olah comes first in the text and lacks an explicit yavdil requirement, supporting the reading that lo yavdil applies only to chatas

macro_segments.title: The Blood of the Olah: Which Component is Essential for Validity

micro_segments.title: Baraita: olah is valid even if only the body's blood is squeezed out, not the head's blood
Question whether the head's blood should be more important, and the textual source (hu) indicating only one component is necessary
Ravina's logical explanation: the majority of blood is in the body, so the body blood is what the Torah emphasizes

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: Chatas Done Like an Olah and Olah Done Like a Chatas—Cases of Invalidation

micro_segments.title: Chatas bird done like an olah (above, with olah intent, or with olah melikah) is invalid regardless of intent or method
Olah bird done correctly (above, like olah method, with olah intent) is valid
Olah done like chatas (one siman instead of two, or wrong blood handling) is invalid
Olah done in the wrong location (below) is invalid regardless of method or intent

macro_segments.title: Gemara Analysis: What Does It Mean to Do a Chatas Like an Olah?

micro_segments.title: Does doing a chatas like an olah mean severing both simanim (creating a melikah dispute) or changing the blood procedure?
Conclusion that the language refers to haza'ah (blood sprinkling) rather than melikah, since melikah can occur anywhere on the altar
Gemara's reasoning using the seifa (end of mishnah) about wrong location to show it discusses blood placement, not melikah location
Distinction that both the end case (location) and the beginning case (doing it like another) focus on blood handling as the key invalidating factor

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: These Invalid Offerings Are Not Treated as Neveilah and Involve Me'ilah

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: invalid chatas and olah birds do not cause tumah like neveilah; me'ilah applies to them
Exception: chatas bird done below like a chatas with chatas intent—debate between Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Yehoshua on me'ilah applicability
Rabbi Eliezer: me'ilah applies even when done entirely like a chatas, because it remains an invalid olah
Rabbi Yehoshua: no me'ilah because complete recategorization as a chatas removes it from me'ilah status

macro_segments.title: Debate between Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Yehoshua: Can an Offering Be Recategorized to Avoid Me'ilah?

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's challenge: if chatas done le-shem olah becomes meilu'ah because recategorized as olah, the same should apply to olah done le-shem chatas
Rabbi Eliezer's reductio ad absurdum using kodshei kodashim (bull, ram, goat) done as kodashim kalim (peace offerings) in the south
Rabbi Yehoshua's response: the reductio fails because peace offerings have me'ilah for entrails, so complete recategorization doesn't help
Rabbi Yehoshua's reasoning: bird chatas is completely exempt from me'ilah (entire bird is eaten), so recategorizing an olah as chatas removes me'ilah

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Eliezer vs. Rabbi Yehoshua (me'ilah debate)
